concepts.title: Relation between Arithmetic Mean and Geometric Mean
concepts.conceptBody:
Arithmetic Means and Geometric Means 

The arithmetic mean, or less precisely the average, of a list of $n$ numbers ${{x}_{1}},{{x}_{2}},\ldots ,{{x}_{n}}$ is the sum of the numbers divided by $\text{n}$ :

$\frac{{{\text{x}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{2}}+\ldots ..+{{\text{x}}_{\text{n}}}}{\text{n}}$

The geometric mean is similar, except that it is only defined for a list of nonnegative real numbers, and uses multiplication and a root in place of addition and division.

$\sqrt[n]{{{\text{x}}_{1}}\cdot {{\text{x}}_{2}}\ldots {{\text{x}}_{\text{n}}}}$

If ${{x}_{1}},{{x}_{2}},\ldots {{x}_{n}}>0$, this is equal to the exponential of the arithmetic mean of the natural logarithms of the numbers :

$\text{exp}\left( \frac{1n{{x}_{1}}+1n{{x}_{2}}+\ldots +1n{{x}_{n}}}{n} \right)$

concepts.title: Weighted AM-GM inequality
concepts.conceptBody:
The AM-GM Inequality 

Restating the inequality using mathematical notation, we have that for any list of $\text{n}$ nonnegative real numbers ${{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{x}}_{2}},\ldots .{{\text{x}}_{\text{n}}}$,

$\frac{{{\text{x}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{2}}+\ldots .+{{\text{x}}_{\text{n}}}}{\text{n}}\ge \sqrt[n]{{{\text{x}}_{1}}\cdot {{\text{x}}_{2}}\ldots {{\text{x}}_{\text{n}}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ (known }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ AM-GM }\!\!~\!\!\text{ inequality) }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ AM}\ge \text{GM}$

and that equality holds if and only if ${{x}_{1}}={{x}_{2}}=\ldots {{x}_{n}}$.

Generalizations 

There is a similar inequality for the weighted arithmetic mean and weighted geometric mean. Specially, let the nonnegative numbers ${{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{x}}_{2}},\ldots ..,{{\text{x}}_{\text{n}}}$ and the nonnegative weights ${{\text{a}}_{1}},{{\text{a}}_{2}},\ldots ...{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}$ be given. Set $\alpha ={{\alpha }_{1}}+{{\alpha }_{2}}+\ldots ..+{{\alpha }_{n}}$. If $\alpha >0$, then the inequality

$\frac{{{\alpha }_{1}}{{x}_{1}}+{{\alpha }_{2}}{{x}_{2}}+\ldots +{{a}_{n}}{{x}_{n}}}{\alpha }\ge \sqrt[\alpha ]{x_{1}^{{{\alpha }_{1}}}x_{2}^{{{\alpha }_{2}}}\ldots x_{n}^{{{a}_{n}}}}$

holds with equality if and only if all the ${{x}_{k}}$ with ak $>0$ are equal. Here the convention $0=1$ is used. If all ${{\text{a}}_{\text{k}}}=1$, this reduces to the above AM-GM inequality.

concepts.title: Relation between Arithmetic and Geometric Mean for the mth power of a sequence of numbers
concepts.conceptBody:
Arithmetic mean of ${{\text{m}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ power 

Let ${{a}_{1}},{{a}_{2}}\ldots \ldots {{a}_{n}}$ be $n$ positive real numbers and let $m$ be a real number, then

$\frac{\text{a}_{1}^{\text{m}}+\text{a}_{2}^{\text{m}}+\ldots ..\text{a}_{\text{n}}^{\text{m}}}{\text{n}}\ge {{\left( \frac{{{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{2}}+\ldots .{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}}{\text{n}} \right)}^{\text{m}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ if }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}\in \text{R}-\left[ 0,1 \right]$

for $\text{m}\in \left( 0,1 \right)$, then $\frac{\text{a}_{1}^{\text{m}}+\text{a}_{2}^{\text{m}}+\ldots .\text{a}_{\text{n}}^{\text{m}}}{\text{n}}\le {{\left( \frac{{{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{2}}+\ldots .{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}}{\text{n}} \right)}^{\text{m}}}$

and for $\text{m}=0~\And~ 1\frac{\text{a}_{1}^{\text{m}}+\text{a}_{2}^{\text{m}}+\ldots .\text{a}_{\text{n}}^{\text{m}}}{\text{n}}={{\left( \frac{{{\text{a}}_{1}}+{{\text{a}}_{2}}+\ldots .{{\text{a}}_{\text{n}}}}{\text{n}} \right)}^{\text{m}}}$

Q) Prove that ${{a}^{4}}+{{b}^{4}}+{{c}^{4}}\ge abc\left( a+b+c \right)\left[ {{a},}{{b},}c>0 \right]$ 

Solution : 

Using ${{\text{m}}^{\text{th }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}$ power inequality

$\frac{{{a}^{4}}+{{b}^{4}}+{{c}^{4}}}{3}\ge {{\left( \frac{a+b+c}{3} \right)}^{4}}$ (i)

Now we have ${{\left( \frac{a+b+c}{3} \right)}^{4}}$ in RHS

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ using }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ AM }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }>\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ GM }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{a+b+c}{3}\geq {{(abc)}^{1/3}} \\ \Rightarrow & {{\left( \frac{a+b+c}{3} \right)}^{3}}\geq abc \\ \end{array}$

but we require ${{\left( \frac{a+b+c}{3} \right)}^{4}}$, so multiplying both sides by $\frac{\left( a+b+c \right)}{3}$

${{\left( \frac{a+b+c}{3} \right)}^{4}}\ge \frac{abc\left( a+b+c \right)}{3}$ (ii)

From (i) & (ii) we get

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \frac{{{a}^{4}}+{{b}^{4}}+{{c}^{4}}}{3}\ge \frac{abc\left( a+b+c \right)}{3} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } & {{a}^{4}}+{{b}^{4}}+{{c}^{4}}\ge abc\left( a+b+c \right) \\ \end{array}$

Hence proved.
